revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Ono Jinja
小野神社 | image = Tama-Ono-jinja haiden.jpeg | caption = Haiden of Ono Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|10.81|N|139|26|32.18|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1-18-8 Ichinomiya, Tama-shi, Tokyo-to | website = | architecture_style = | festival = second Sunday of September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ono Jinja'''|小野神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tama, Tokyo|Tama]] in [[Tokyo Metropolis]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Musashi Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the second Sunday of September. In the [[Edo Period]], it was also called the {{nihongo|Ichinomiya Daimyōjin|一宮大明神||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ono Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-shitaharu-no-Mikoto'''| 天下春命|}}, ancestor of the [[Chichibu Province|Chichibu]] ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' * {{nihongo|'''Seoritsu-hime no Mikoto'''| 瀬織津比咩命|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Izanagi|Izanagi no Mikoto]]'''| 伊弉諾尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]'''| 素盞嗚尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴大神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ninigi]]'''| 瓊々杵尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori |Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ukanomitama]]'''|倉稲魂命|}} ==History== The origins of Ono Jinja are unknown. The site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Musashi Province are located nearby, and the shrine first appears in the historical record in 772, followed by a mention in the [[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] in 884, and in the [[Engishiki]], where it is listed as a minor shrine. It is styled as the "ichinomiya" of Musashi in the early [[Kamakura period]] ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' and in the [[Nanboku-chō period]] [[Shintōshū]].The shrine was rebuilt in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Ota Dokan]]and received a stipend in the [[Edo Period]] from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a county shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] The shrine is located a six-minute walk from [[Seiseki-Sakuragaoka Station]] on the [[Keio Electric Railway]] [[Keio Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== 小野神社 (多摩市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 小野神社 (多摩市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden' 小野神社 (多摩市) 随神門.JPG|Zuishin-mon ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons category-inline|Ono-jinja (Tama)}} *[http://onojinja.or.jp Official homepage] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Tama, Tokyo]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Ono Jinja
小野神社 | image = Tama-Ono-jinja haiden.jpeg | caption = Haiden of Ono Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|10.81|N|139|26|32.18|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1-18-8 Ichinomiya, Tama-shi, Tokyo-to | website = | architecture_style = | festival = second Sunday of September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ono Jinja'''|小野神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tama, Tokyo|Tama]] in [[Tokyo Metropolis]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Musashi Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the second Sunday of September. During the [[Edo Period]], it was also called the {{nihongo|Ichinomiya Daimyōjin|一宮大明神||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ono Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-shitaharu-no-Mikoto'''| 天下春命|}}, ancestor of the [[Chichibu Province|Chichibu]] ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' * {{nihongo|'''Seoritsu-hime no Mikoto'''| 瀬織津比咩命|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Izanagi|Izanagi no Mikoto]]'''| 伊弉諾尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]'''| 素盞嗚尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴大神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ninigi]]'''| 瓊々杵尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori |Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ukanomitama]]'''|倉稲魂命|}} ==History== The origins of Ono Jinja are unknown. The site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Musashi Province are located nearby, and the shrine first appears in the historical record in 772, followed by a mention in the [[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] in 884, and in the [[Engishiki]], where it is listed as a minor shrine. It is styled as the "ichinomiya" of Musashi in the early [[Kamakura period]] ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' and in the [[Nanboku-chō period]] [[Shintōshū]].The shrine was rebuilt in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Ota Dokan]]and received a stipend in the [[Edo Period]] from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a county shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] The shrine is located a six-minute walk from [[Seiseki-Sakuragaoka Station]] on the [[Keio Electric Railway]] [[Keio Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== 小野神社 (多摩市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 小野神社 (多摩市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden' 小野神社 (多摩市) 随神門.JPG|Zuishin-mon ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons category-inline|Ono-jinja (Tama)}} *[http://onojinja.or.jp Official homepage] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Tama, Tokyo]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]]

The site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Musashi Province are located nearby, and the shrine first appears in the historical record in 772, followed by a mention in the [[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] in 884, and in the [[Engishiki]], where it is listed as a minor shrine. It is styled as the "ichinomiya" of Musashi in the early [[Kamakura period]] ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' and in the [[Nanboku-chō period]] [[Shintōshū]].The shrine was rebuilt in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Ota Dokan]] and received a stipend in the [[Edo Period]] from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a county shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is located a six-minute walk from [[Seiseki-Sakuragaoka Station]] on the [[Keio Electric Railway]] [[Keio Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== 小野神社 (多摩市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 小野神社 (多摩市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden' 小野神社 (多摩市) 随神門.JPG|Zuishin-mon ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons category-inline|Ono-jinja (Tama)}} *[http://onojinja.or.jp Official homepage] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Tama, Tokyo]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Tokyo Metropolitan Designated Tangible Cultural Property]]

It is styled as the "ichinomiya" of Musashi in the early [[Kamakura period]] ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' and in the [[Nanboku-chō period]] [[Shintōshū]].The shrine was rebuilt in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Ota Dokan]] and received a stipend in the [[Edo Period]] from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a county shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is located a six-minute walk from [[Seiseki-Sakuragaoka Station]] on the [[Keio Electric Railway]] [[Keio Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== 小野神社 (多摩市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 小野神社 (多摩市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden' 小野神社 (多摩市) 随神門.JPG|Zuishin-mon ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons category-inline|Ono-jinja (Tama)}} *[http://onojinja.or.jp Official homepage] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Tama, Tokyo]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Tokyo Metropolitan Designated Tangible Cultural Property]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[category:dd]] [[en:Ono Shrine]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Ono Jinja
小野神社 | image = Tama-Ono-jinja haiden.jpeg | caption = Haiden of Ono Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|10.81|N|139|26|32.18|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1-18-8 Ichinomiya, Tama-shi, Tokyo-to | website = | architecture_style = | festival = second Sunday of September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ono Jinja'''|小野神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tama, Tokyo|Tama]] in [[Tokyo Metropolis]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Musashi Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the second Sunday of September. During the [[Edo Period]], it was also called the {{nihongo|Ichinomiya Daimyōjin|一宮大明神||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ono Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-shitaharu-no-Mikoto'''| 天下春命|}}, ancestor of the [[Chichibu Province|Chichibu]] ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' * {{nihongo|'''Seoritsu-hime no Mikoto'''| 瀬織津比咩命|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Izanagi|Izanagi no Mikoto]]'''| 伊弉諾尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]'''| 素盞嗚尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴大神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ninigi]]'''| 瓊々杵尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori |Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ukanomitama]]'''|倉稲魂命|}} ==History== The origins of Ono Jinja are unknown. The site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Musashi Province are located nearby, and the shrine first appears in the historical record in 772, followed by a mention in the [[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] in 884, and in the [[Engishiki]], where it is listed as a minor shrine. It is styled as the "ichinomiya" of Musashi in the early [[Kamakura period]] ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' and in the [[Nanboku-chō period]] [[Shintōshū]].The shrine was rebuilt in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Ota Dokan]] and received a stipend in the [[Edo Period]] from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a county shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is located a six-minute walk from [[Seiseki-Sakuragaoka Station]] on the [[Keio Electric Railway]] [[Keio Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== 小野神社 (多摩市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 小野神社 (多摩市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden' 小野神社 (多摩市) 随神門.JPG|Zuishin-mon ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons category-inline|Ono-jinja (Tama)}} *[http://onojinja.or.jp Official homepage] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Tama, Tokyo]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Tokyo Metropolitan Designated Tangible Cultural Property]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[en:Ono Shrine]]

It is styled as the "ichinomiya" of Musashi in the early [[Kamakura period]] ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' and in the [[Nanboku-chō period]] [[Shintōshū]].The shrine was rebuilt in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Ota Dokan]] and received a stipend in the [[Edo Period]] from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a county shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is located a six-minute walk from [[Seiseki-Sakuragaoka Station]] on the [[Keio Electric Railway]] [[Keio Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== 小野神社 (多摩市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 小野神社 (多摩市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden' 小野神社 (多摩市) 随神門.JPG|Zuishin-mon ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons category-inline|Ono-jinja (Tama)}} *[http://onojinja.or.jp Official homepage] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Tama, Tokyo]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Tokyo Metropolitan Designated Tangible Cultural Property]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Ono Shrine]][[en:Ono Shrine]]

The site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Musashi Province are located nearby, and the shrine first appears in the historical record in 772, followed by a mention in the [[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] in 884, and in the [[Engishiki]], where it is listed as a minor shrine. The shrine is located a six-minute walk from [[Seiseki-Sakuragaoka Station]] on the [[Keio Electric Railway]] [[Keio Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== 小野神社 (多摩市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 小野神社 (多摩市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden' 小野神社 (多摩市) 随神門.JPG|Zuishin-mon ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Musashi Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the second Sunday of September. During the [[Edo Period]], it was also called the {{nihongo|Ichinomiya Daimyōjin|一宮大明神||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ono Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-shitaharu-no-Mikoto'''| 天下春命|}}, ancestor of the [[Chichibu Province|Chichibu]] ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' * {{nihongo|'''Seoritsu-hime no Mikoto'''| 瀬織津比咩命|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Izanagi|Izanagi no Mikoto]]'''| 伊弉諾尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]'''| 素盞嗚尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴大神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ninigi]]'''| 瓊々杵尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori |Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ukanomitama]]'''|倉稲魂命|}} ==History== The origins of Ono Jinja are unknown. The site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Musashi Province are located nearby, and the shrine first appears in the historical record in 772, followed by a mention in the [[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] in 884, and in the [[Engishiki]], where it is listed as a minor shrine. It is styled as the "ichinomiya" of Musashi in the early [[Kamakura period]] ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' and in the [[Nanboku-chō period]] [[Shintōshū]].The shrine was rebuilt in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Ota Dokan]] and received a stipend in the [[Edo Period]] from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a county shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is located a six-minute walk from [[Seiseki-Sakuragaoka Station]] on the [[Keio Electric Railway]] [[Keio Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== 小野神社 (多摩市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 小野神社 (多摩市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden' 小野神社 (多摩市) 随神門.JPG|Zuishin-mon ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category-inline|Ono-jinja (Tama)}} *[http://onojinja.or.jp Official homepage] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Tama, Tokyo]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Tokyo Metropolitan Designated Tangible Cultural Property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:dardar]] [[Category:Images]] [[en:Ono Shrine]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Ono Jinja
小野神社 | image = Tama-Ono-jinja haiden.jpeg | caption = Haiden of Ono Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|10.81|N|139|26|32.18|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1-18-8 Ichinomiya, Tama-shi, Tokyo-to | website = | architecture_style = | festival = second Sunday of September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ono Jinja'''|小野神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tama, Tokyo|Tama]] in [[Tokyo Metropolis]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Musashi Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the second Sunday of September. During the [[Edo Period]], it was also called the {{nihongo|Ichinomiya Daimyōjin|一宮大明神||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ono Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-shitaharu-no-Mikoto'''| 天下春命|}}, ancestor of the [[Chichibu Province|Chichibu]] ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' * {{nihongo|'''Seoritsu-hime no Mikoto'''| 瀬織津比咩命|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Izanagi|Izanagi no Mikoto]]'''| 伊弉諾尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]'''| 素盞嗚尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴大神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ninigi]]'''| 瓊々杵尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori |Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ukanomitama]]'''|倉稲魂命|}} ==History== The origins of Ono Jinja are unknown. The site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Musashi Province are located nearby, and the shrine first appears in the historical record in 772, followed by a mention in the [[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] in 884, and in the [[Engishiki]], where it is listed as a minor shrine. It is styled as the "ichinomiya" of Musashi in the early [[Kamakura period]] ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' and in the [[Nanboku-chō period]] [[Shintōshū]].The shrine was rebuilt in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Ota Dokan]] and received a stipend in the [[Edo Period]] from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a county shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is located a six-minute walk from [[Seiseki-Sakuragaoka Station]] on the [[Keio Electric Railway]] [[Keio Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== 小野神社 (多摩市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 小野神社 (多摩市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden' 小野神社 (多摩市) 随神門.JPG|Zuishin-mon ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category-inline|Ono-jinja (Tama)}} *[http://onojinja.or.jp Official homepage] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tama, Tokyo]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tokyo Metropolitan Designated Tangible Cultural Property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Ono Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[en:Ono Shrine]]

[http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category-inline|Ono-jinja (Tama)}} *[http://onojinja.or.jp Official homepage] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tama, Tokyo]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tokyo Metropolitan Designated Tangible Cultural Property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Ono Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[en:Ono Shrine]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Ono Jinja
小野神社 | image = Tama-Ono-jinja haiden.jpeg | caption = Haiden of Ono Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|10.81|N|139|26|32.18|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1-18-8 Ichinomiya, Tama-shi, Tokyo-to | website = | architecture_style = | festival = second Sunday of September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ono Jinja'''|小野神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tama, Tokyo|Tama]] in [[Tokyo Metropolis]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Musashi Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the second Sunday of September. During the [[Edo Period]], it was also called the {{nihongo|Ichinomiya Daimyōjin|一宮大明神||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ono Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-shitaharu-no-Mikoto'''| 天下春命|}}, ancestor of the [[Chichibu Province|Chichibu]] ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' * {{nihongo|'''Seoritsu-hime no Mikoto'''| 瀬織津比咩命|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Izanagi|Izanagi no Mikoto]]'''| 伊弉諾尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]'''| 素盞嗚尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴大神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ninigi]]'''| 瓊々杵尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori |Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ukanomitama]]'''|倉稲魂命|}} ==History== The origins of Ono Jinja are unknown. The site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Musashi Province are located nearby, and the shrine first appears in the historical record in 772, followed by a mention in the [[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] in 884, and in the [[Engishiki]], where it is listed as a minor shrine. It is styled as the "ichinomiya" of Musashi in the early [[Kamakura period]] ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' and in the [[Nanboku-chō period]] [[Shintōshū]].The shrine was rebuilt in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Ota Dokan]] and received a stipend in the [[Edo Period]] from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a county shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is located a six-minute walk from [[Seiseki-Sakuragaoka Station]] on the [[Keio Electric Railway]] [[Keio Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== 小野神社 (多摩市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 小野神社 (多摩市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden' 小野神社 (多摩市) 随神門.JPG|Zuishin-mon ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category-inline|Ono-jinja (Tama)}} *[http://onojinja.or.jp Official homepage] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Ono Shrine]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tama, Tokyo]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Tokyo Metropolitan Designated Tangible Cultural Property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Ono Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[en:Ono Shrine]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Ono Jinja
小野神社 | image = Tama-Ono-jinja haiden.jpeg | caption = Haiden of Ono Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|10.81|N|139|26|32.18|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1-18-8 Ichinomiya, Tama-shi, Tokyo-to | website = | architecture_style = | festival = second Sunday of September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ono Jinja'''|小野神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tama, Tokyo|Tama]] in [[Tokyo Metropolis]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Musashi Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the second Sunday of September. During the [[Edo Period]], it was also called the {{nihongo|Ichinomiya Daimyōjin|一宮大明神||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ono Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-shitaharu-no-Mikoto'''| 天下春命|}}, ancestor of the [[Chichibu Province|Chichibu]] ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' * {{nihongo|'''Seoritsu-hime no Mikoto'''| 瀬織津比咩命|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Izanagi|Izanagi no Mikoto]]'''| 伊弉諾尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]'''| 素盞嗚尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴大神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ninigi]]'''| 瓊々杵尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori |Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ukanomitama]]'''|倉稲魂命|}} ==History== The origins of Ono Jinja are unknown. The site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Musashi Province are located nearby, and the shrine first appears in the historical record in 772, followed by a mention in the [[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] in 884, and in the [[Engishiki]], where it is listed as a minor shrine. It is styled as the "ichinomiya" of Musashi in the early [[Kamakura period]] ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' and in the [[Nanboku-chō period]] [[Shintōshū]].The shrine was rebuilt in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Ota Dokan]] and received a stipend in the [[Edo Period]] from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a county shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is located a six-minute walk from [[Seiseki-Sakuragaoka Station]] on the [[Keio Electric Railway]] [[Keio Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== 小野神社 (多摩市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 小野神社 (多摩市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden' 小野神社 (多摩市) 随神門.JPG|Zuishin-mon ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Ono Jinja
小野神社 | image = Tama-Ono-jinja haiden.jpeg | caption = Haiden of Ono Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|10.81|N|139|26|32.18|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1-18-8 Ichinomiya, Tama-shi, Tokyo-to | website = | architecture_style = | festival = second Sunday of September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ono Jinja'''|小野神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tama, Tokyo|Tama]] in [[Tokyo Metropolis]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Musashi Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the second Sunday of September. During the [[Edo Period]], it was also called the {{nihongo|Ichinomiya Daimyōjin|一宮大明神||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ono Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-shitaharu-no-Mikoto'''| 天下春命|}}, ancestor of the [[Chichibu Province|Chichibu]] ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' * {{nihongo|'''Seoritsu-hime no Mikoto'''| 瀬織津比咩命|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Izanagi|Izanagi no Mikoto]]'''| 伊弉諾尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]'''| 素盞嗚尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴大神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ninigi]]'''| 瓊々杵尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori |Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ukanomitama]]'''|倉稲魂命|}} ==History== The origins of Ono Jinja are unknown. The site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Musashi Province are located nearby, and the shrine first appears in the historical record in 772, followed by a mention in the [[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] in 884, and in the [[Engishiki]], where it is listed as a minor shrine. It is styled as the "ichinomiya" of Musashi in the early [[Kamakura period]] ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' and in the [[Nanboku-chō period]] [[Shintōshū]].The shrine was rebuilt in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Ota Dokan]] and received a stipend in the [[Edo Period]] from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a county shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is located a six-minute walk from [[Seiseki-Sakuragaoka Station]] on the [[Keio Electric Railway]] [[Keio Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== 小野神社 (多摩市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 小野神社 (多摩市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden' 小野神社 (多摩市) 随神門.JPG|Zuishin-mon ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. It is styled as the "ichinomiya" of Musashi in the early [[Kamakura period]] ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' and in the [[Nanboku-chō period]] [[Shintōshū]].The shrine was rebuilt in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Ota Dokan]] and received a stipend in the [[Edo Period]] from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a county shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is located a six-minute walk from [[Seiseki-Sakuragaoka Station]] on the [[Keio Electric Railway]] [[Keio Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== 小野神社 (多摩市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 小野神社 (多摩市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden' 小野神社 (多摩市) 随神門.JPG|Zuishin-mon ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. {{Infobox religious building | name = Ono Jinja
小野神社 | image = Tama-Ono-jinja haiden.jpeg | caption = Haiden of Ono Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|10.81|N|139|26|32.18|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1-18-8 Ichinomiya, Tama-shi, Tokyo-to | website = | architecture_style = | festival = second Sunday of September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ono Jinja'''|小野神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tama, Tokyo|Tama]] in [[Tokyo Metropolis]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Musashi Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the second Sunday of September. During the [[Edo Period]], it was also called the {{nihongo|Ichinomiya Daimyōjin|一宮大明神||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ono Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-shitaharu-no-Mikoto'''| 天下春命|}}, ancestor of the [[Chichibu Province|Chichibu]] ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' * {{nihongo|'''Seoritsu-hime no Mikoto'''| 瀬織津比咩命|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Izanagi|Izanagi no Mikoto]]'''| 伊弉諾尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]'''| 素盞嗚尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴大神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ninigi]]'''| 瓊々杵尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori |Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ukanomitama]]'''|倉稲魂命|}} ==History== The origins of Ono Jinja are unknown. The site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Musashi Province are located nearby, and the shrine first appears in the historical record in 772, followed by a mention in the [[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] in 884, and in the [[Engishiki]], where it is listed as a minor shrine. It is styled as the "ichinomiya" of Musashi in the early [[Kamakura period]] ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' and in the [[Nanboku-chō period]] [[Shintōshū]].The shrine was rebuilt in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Ota Dokan]] and received a stipend in the [[Edo Period]] from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a county shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is located a six-minute walk from [[Seiseki-Sakuragaoka Station]] on the [[Keio Electric Railway]] [[Keio Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== 小野神社 (多摩市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 小野神社 (多摩市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden' 小野神社 (多摩市) 随神門.JPG|Zuishin-mon ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. {{Infobox religious building | name = Ono Jinja
小野神社 | image = Tama-Ono-jinja haiden.jpeg | caption = Haiden of Ono Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|10.81|N|139|26|32.18|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1-18-8 Ichinomiya, Tama-shi, Tokyo-to | website = | architecture_style = | festival = second Sunday of September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ono Jinja'''|小野神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tama, Tokyo|Tama]] in [[Tokyo Metropolis]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Musashi Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the second Sunday of September. During the [[Edo Period]], it was also called the {{nihongo|Ichinomiya Daimyōjin|一宮大明神||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ono Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-shitaharu-no-Mikoto'''| 天下春命|}}, ancestor of the [[Chichibu Province|Chichibu]] ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' * {{nihongo|'''Seoritsu-hime no Mikoto'''| 瀬織津比咩命|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Izanagi|Izanagi no Mikoto]]'''| 伊弉諾尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]'''| 素盞嗚尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴大神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ninigi]]'''| 瓊々杵尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori |Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ukanomitama]]'''|倉稲魂命|}} ==History== The origins of Ono Jinja are unknown. The site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Musashi Province are located nearby, and the shrine first appears in the historical record in 772, followed by a mention in the [[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] in 884, and in the [[Engishiki]], where it is listed as a minor shrine. It is styled as the "ichinomiya" of Musashi in the early [[Kamakura period]] ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' and in the [[Nanboku-chō period]] [[Shintōshū]].The shrine was rebuilt in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Ota Dokan]] and received a stipend in the [[Edo Period]] from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a county shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is located a six-minute walk from [[Seiseki-Sakuragaoka Station]] on the [[Keio Electric Railway]] [[Keio Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== 小野神社 (多摩市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 小野神社 (多摩市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden' 小野神社 (多摩市) 随神門.JPG|Zuishin-mon ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia {{Infobox religious building | name = Ono Jinja
小野神社 | image = Tama-Ono-jinja haiden.jpeg | caption = Haiden of Ono Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|10.81|N|139|26|32.18|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1-18-8 Ichinomiya, Tama-shi, Tokyo-to | website = | architecture_style = | festival = second Sunday of September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ono Jinja'''|小野神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tama, Tokyo|Tama]] in [[Tokyo Metropolis]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Musashi Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the second Sunday of September. During the [[Edo Period]], it was also called the {{nihongo|Ichinomiya Daimyōjin|一宮大明神||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ono Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-shitaharu-no-Mikoto'''| 天下春命|}}, ancestor of the [[Chichibu Province|Chichibu]] ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' * {{nihongo|'''Seoritsu-hime no Mikoto'''| 瀬織津比咩命|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Izanagi|Izanagi no Mikoto]]'''| 伊弉諾尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]'''| 素盞嗚尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴大神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ninigi]]'''| 瓊々杵尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori |Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ukanomitama]]'''|倉稲魂命|}} ==History== The origins of Ono Jinja are unknown. The site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Musashi Province are located nearby, and the shrine first appears in the historical record in 772, followed by a mention in the [[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] in 884, and in the [[Engishiki]], where it is listed as a minor shrine. It is styled as the "ichinomiya" of Musashi in the early [[Kamakura period]] ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' and in the [[Nanboku-chō period]] [[Shintōshū]].The shrine was rebuilt in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Ota Dokan]] and received a stipend in the [[Edo Period]] from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a county shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is located a six-minute walk from [[Seiseki-Sakuragaoka Station]] on the [[Keio Electric Railway]] [[Keio Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== 小野神社 (多摩市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 小野神社 (多摩市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden' 小野神社 (多摩市) 随神門.JPG|Zuishin-mon ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:qq]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Musashi Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the second Sunday of September. During the [[Edo Period]], it was also called the {{nihongo|Ichinomiya Daimyōjin|一宮大明神||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ono Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-shitaharu-no-Mikoto'''| 天下春命|}}, ancestor of the [[Chichibu Province|Chichibu]] ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' * {{nihongo|'''Seoritsu-hime no Mikoto'''| 瀬織津比咩命|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Izanagi|Izanagi no Mikoto]]'''| 伊弉諾尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]'''| 素盞嗚尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴大神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ninigi]]'''| 瓊々杵尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori |Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ukanomitama]]'''|倉稲魂命|}} ==History== The origins of Ono Jinja are unknown. The site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Musashi Province are located nearby, and the shrine first appears in the historical record in 772, followed by a mention in the [[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] in 884, and in the [[Engishiki]], where it is listed as a minor shrine. It is styled as the "ichinomiya" of Musashi in the early [[Kamakura period]] ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' and in the [[Nanboku-chō period]] [[Shintōshū]].The shrine was rebuilt in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Ota Dokan]] and received a stipend in the [[Edo Period]] from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a county shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is located a six-minute walk from [[Seiseki-Sakuragaoka Station]] on the [[Keio Electric Railway]] [[Keio Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== 小野神社 (多摩市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 小野神社 (多摩市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden' 小野神社 (多摩市) 随神門.JPG|Zuishin-mon ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ono Shrine]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ono Shrine]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category-inline|Ono-jinja (Tama)}} *[http://onojinja.or.jp Official homepage] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Ono Shrine]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ono Shrine]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ono Shrine]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ono Shrine]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:Tama, Tokyo]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ono Shrine]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ono Shrine]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:Tokyo Metropolitan Designated Tangible Cultural Property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ono Shrine]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ono Shrine]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:Ono Shrine]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ono Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[en:Ono Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] {{Ichinomiya}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Ono Jinja
小野神社 | image = Tama-Ono-jinja haiden.jpeg | caption = Haiden of Ono Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|10.81|N|139|26|32.18|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1-18-8 Ichinomiya, Tama-shi, Tokyo-to | website = | architecture_style = | festival = second Sunday of September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ono Jinja'''|小野神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tama, Tokyo|Tama]] in [[Tokyo Metropolis]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Musashi Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the second Sunday of September. During the [[Edo Period]], it was also called the {{nihongo|Ichinomiya Daimyōjin|一宮大明神||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ono Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-shitaharu-no-Mikoto'''| 天下春命|}}, ancestor of the [[Chichibu Province|Chichibu]] ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' * {{nihongo|'''Seoritsu-hime no Mikoto'''| 瀬織津比咩命|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Izanagi|Izanagi no Mikoto]]'''| 伊弉諾尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]'''| 素盞嗚尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴大神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ninigi]]'''| 瓊々杵尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori |Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ukanomitama]]'''|倉稲魂命|}} ==History== The origins of Ono Jinja are unknown. The site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Musashi Province are located nearby, and the shrine first appears in the historical record in 772, followed by a mention in the [[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] in 884, and in the [[Engishiki]], where it is listed as a minor shrine. It is styled as the "ichinomiya" of Musashi in the early [[Kamakura period]] ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' and in the [[Nanboku-chō period]] [[Shintōshū]].The shrine was rebuilt in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Ota Dokan]] and received a stipend in the [[Edo Period]] from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a county shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is located a six-minute walk from [[Seiseki-Sakuragaoka Station]] on the [[Keio Electric Railway]] [[Keio Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== 小野神社 (多摩市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 小野神社 (多摩市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden' 小野神社 (多摩市) 随神門.JPG|Zuishin-mon ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ono Shrine]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ono Shrine]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category-inline|Ono-jinja (Tama)}} *[http://onojinja.or.jp Official homepage] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]][[en:Ono Shrine]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ono Shrine]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ono Shrine]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ono Shrine]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:Tama, Tokyo]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ono Shrine]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ono Shrine]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:Tokyo Metropolitan Designated Tangible Cultural Property]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ono Shrine]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]][[Category:11]] [[en:Ono Shrine]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[en:Ono Shrine]][[Category:Ono Shrine]][[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Ono Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[en:Ono Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Ono Shrine]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] {{Ichinomiya}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Ono Jinja
小野神社 | image = Tama-Ono-jinja haiden.jpeg | caption = Haiden of Ono Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|10.81|N|139|26|32.18|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1-18-8 Ichinomiya, Tama-shi, Tokyo-to | website = | architecture_style = | festival = second Sunday of September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ono Jinja'''|小野神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tama, Tokyo|Tama]] in [[Tokyo Metropolis]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Musashi Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the second Sunday of September. During the [[Edo Period]], it was also called the {{nihongo|Ichinomiya Daimyōjin|一宮大明神||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ono Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-shitaharu-no-Mikoto'''| 天下春命|}}, ancestor of the [[Chichibu Province|Chichibu]] ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' * {{nihongo|'''Seoritsu-hime no Mikoto'''| 瀬織津比咩命|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Izanagi|Izanagi no Mikoto]]'''| 伊弉諾尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]'''| 素盞嗚尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴大神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ninigi]]'''| 瓊々杵尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori |Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ukanomitama]]'''|倉稲魂命|}} ==History== The origins of Ono Jinja are unknown. The site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Musashi Province are located nearby, and the shrine first appears in the historical record in 772, followed by a mention in the [[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] in 884, and in the [[Engishiki]], where it is listed as a minor shrine. It is styled as the "ichinomiya" of Musashi in the early [[Kamakura period]] ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' and in the [[Nanboku-chō period]] [[Shintōshū]].The shrine was rebuilt in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Ota Dokan]] and received a stipend in the [[Edo Period]] from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a county shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is located a six-minute walk from [[Seiseki-Sakuragaoka Station]] on the [[Keio Electric Railway]] [[Keio Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== 小野神社 (多摩市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 小野神社 (多摩市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden' 小野神社 (多摩市) 随神門.JPG|Zuishin-mon ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category-inline|Ono-jinja (Tama)}} *[http://onojinja.or.jp Official homepage] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Tama, Tokyo]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Tokyo Metropolitan Designated Tangible Cultural Property]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Ono Shrine]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Ono Shrine]] [[sn:Ono Shrine]]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category-inline|Ono-jinja (Tama)}} *[http://onojinja.or.jp Official homepage] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Tama, Tokyo]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Tokyo Metropolitan Designated Tangible Cultural Property]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Ono Shrine]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Ono Shrine]] [[sn:Ono Shrine]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Ono Jinja
小野神社 | image = Tama-Ono-jinja haiden.jpeg | caption = Haiden of Ono Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|10.81|N|139|26|32.18|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1-18-8 Ichinomiya, Tama-shi, Tokyo-to | website = | architecture_style = | festival = second Sunday of September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ono Jinja'''|小野神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tama, Tokyo|Tama]] in [[Tokyo Metropolis]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Musashi Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the second Sunday of September. During the [[Edo Period]], it was also called the {{nihongo|Ichinomiya Daimyōjin|一宮大明神||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ono Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-shitaharu-no-Mikoto'''| 天下春命|}}, ancestor of the [[Chichibu Province|Chichibu]] ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' * {{nihongo|'''Seoritsu-hime no Mikoto'''| 瀬織津比咩命|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Izanagi|Izanagi no Mikoto]]'''| 伊弉諾尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]'''| 素盞嗚尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴大神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ninigi]]'''| 瓊々杵尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori |Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ukanomitama]]'''|倉稲魂命|}} ==History== The origins of Ono Jinja are unknown. The site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Musashi Province are located nearby, and the shrine first appears in the historical record in 772, followed by a mention in the [[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] in 884, and in the [[Engishiki]], where it is listed as a minor shrine. It is styled as the "ichinomiya" of Musashi in the early [[Kamakura period]] ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' and in the [[Nanboku-chō period]] [[Shintōshū]].The shrine was rebuilt in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Ota Dokan]] and received a stipend in the [[Edo Period]] from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a county shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is located a six-minute walk from [[Seiseki-Sakuragaoka Station]] on the [[Keio Electric Railway]] [[Keio Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== 小野神社 (多摩市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 小野神社 (多摩市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden' 小野神社 (多摩市) 随神門.JPG|Zuishin-mon ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category-inline|Ono-jinja (Tama)}} *[http://onojinja.or.jp Official homepage] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Tama, Tokyo]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Tokyo Metropolitan Designated Tangible Cultural Property]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Ono Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Ono Shrine]] [[sn:Ono Shrine]]

The site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Musashi Province are located nearby, and the shrine first appears in the historical record in 772, followed by a mention in the [[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] in 884, and in the [[Engishiki]], where it is listed as a minor shrine. It is styled as the "ichinomiya" of Musashi in the early [[Kamakura period]] ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' and in the [[Nanboku-chō period]] [[Shintōshū]].The shrine was rebuilt in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Ota Dokan]] and received a stipend in the [[Edo Period]] from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a county shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is located a six-minute walk from [[Seiseki-Sakuragaoka Station]] on the [[Keio Electric Railway]] [[Keio Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== 小野神社 (多摩市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 小野神社 (多摩市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden' 小野神社 (多摩市) 随神門.JPG|Zuishin-mon ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category-inline|Ono-jinja (Tama)}} *[http://onojinja.or.jp Official homepage] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Tama, Tokyo]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Tokyo Metropolitan Designated Tangible Cultural Property]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Ono Shrine]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Ono Shrine]] [[sn:Ono Shrine]]

{{Infobox religious building | name = Ono Jinja
小野神社 | image = Tama-Ono-jinja haiden.jpeg | caption = Haiden of Ono Jinja Shrine {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Tokyo#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|39|10.81|N|139|26|32.18|E|region:JP-35_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = | founded_by = | established = unknown | date_destroyed = | location =1-18-8 Ichinomiya, Tama-shi, Tokyo-to | website = | architecture_style = | festival = second Sunday of September | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Ono Jinja'''|小野神社}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] in the Ichinomiya neighborhood of the city of [[Tama, Tokyo|Tama]] in [[Tokyo Metropolis]], [[Japan]]. It is one of the two shrines claiming the title of ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Musashi Province]]. The main festival of the shrine is held annually on the second Sunday of September. During the [[Edo Period]], it was also called the {{nihongo|Ichinomiya Daimyōjin|一宮大明神||}}.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Ono Jinja are: * {{nihongo|'''Ame-no-shitaharu-no-Mikoto'''| 天下春命|}}, ancestor of the [[Chichibu Province|Chichibu]] ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' * {{nihongo|'''Seoritsu-hime no Mikoto'''| 瀬織津比咩命|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Izanagi|Izanagi no Mikoto]]'''| 伊弉諾尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Susanoo|Susanoo no Mikoto]]'''| 素盞嗚尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴大神|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]]'''| 瓊々杵尊|}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Hoori |Hikohohodemi no Mikoto]]'''| 彦火火出見尊 |}} * {{nihongo|'''[[Ukanomitama]]'''|倉稲魂命|}} ==History== The origins of Ono Jinja are unknown. The site of the [[kokufu|provincial capital]] of Musashi Province are located nearby, and the shrine first appears in the historical record in 772, followed by a mention in the [[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]] in 884, and in the [[Engishiki]], where it is listed as a minor shrine. It is styled as the "ichinomiya" of Musashi in the early [[Kamakura period]] ''[[Azuma Kagami]]'' and in the [[Nanboku-chō period]] [[Shintōshū]].The shrine was rebuilt in the [[Sengoku period]] by the [[Late Hōjō clan]] and [[Ota Dokan]] and received a stipend in the [[Edo Period]] from the [[Tokugawa shogunate]].{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} During the [[Meiji period]] era of [[State Shinto]], the shrine was rated as a county shrine under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]]. The shrine is located a six-minute walk from [[Seiseki-Sakuragaoka Station]] on the [[Keio Electric Railway]] [[Keio Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== 小野神社 (多摩市) 本殿.JPG|Honden 小野神社 (多摩市) 拝殿.JPG|Haiden' 小野神社 (多摩市) 随神門.JPG|Zuishin-mon ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== * Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ==External links== {{commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category-inline|Ono-jinja (Tama)}} *[http://onojinja.or.jp Official homepage] ==Notes== {{reflist}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Ichinomiya}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Musashi Province]] [[Category:Tama, Tokyo]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Tokyo Metropolitan Designated Tangible Cultural Property]] [[Category:Gō-sha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Ono Shrine]] [[sn:Ono Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]
